Facts.text: Batavian nobleman Gaius Julius Civilis proclaimed emperor of Syria
The Battle of Abrittus in the Balkans won by the Goths against the Romans. Roman Emperors Decius and Herennius Etruscus are killed
Pope Martinus I elected to succeed Theodore I
1st Crusaders defeat Sultan Kilidj Arslan of Nicea
In China, sunglasses are invented
Otto II, Count of Guelders grants Arnhem the status of a city, it had previously belonged to the abbey of Prüm
Duke Frederick II of Austria issues a charter extending rights and protection for Jews - 1st territorial ruler to claim Jews as his own subjects
King Alfonso X 'the Wise', King of Castile & Leon crowned
Battle at Westkapelle - Floris de Voogd 'the Guardian' defeats Guy of Dampierre
Engagement of Count Louis of Male to Margaretha, daughter of Jan III
French and Genuese armada sails out against barbarian pirates
1st burning of Protestants at the stake in the Netherlands
Inquisitor Adrian Boeyens (pope Adrianus VI) becomes cardinal
England & Scotland sign peace treaty of Greenwich

Lithuanian Parliament accepts Union of Lublin, merging the Crown of the Kingdom of Poland and the Grand Duchy of Lithuania into the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, becoming one of Europe's largest countries at the time
Dutch expedition of Jacques Le Maire and Willem Corneliszoon Schouten returns to Zeeland after two years - discovered Cape Horn route around South America (Le Maire died on return voyage)
Siege of Warsaw: Polish King Jan II Casimir recaptures Warsaw from Swedish occupiers
Spain, France & Netherlands form Triple Alliance
Battle at Lafeld: France force beats British and Dutch army in Belgium during the Austrian War of Succession
American privateers attack Lunenburg, Nova Scotia
John Rutledge becomes 2nd chief justice of US Supreme Court
Louis Napoleon abdicates as king of the Netherlands
French frigate Medusa wrecked; basis of Géricault's painting "Raft of the Medusa"
1st edition of newspaper "Courrier de la Meuse" published in the Netherlands
9th US Postmaster General: John McLean of Ohio takes office; appointed by President Monroe on June 26, not confirmed by US Senate until December 9)
United Provinces of Central America declares itself independent of Mexico and Spain
Admiral James C Ross reaches magnetic North Pole
1st US postage stamps go on sale, 5 cent Franklin and 10 cent Washington, NYC
At least 626 ships lie at anchor around San Francisco Bay
1st Canadian coins minted (1, 5, 10 and 20 cent) at the Royal Mint in London, England
1st intercollegiate baseball game, Amherst beats Williams 66-32 in Pittsfield, Massachusetts
Balloon covers a record 809 miles over St Louis
1st public schoolhouse opens at Washington and Mason St, San Francisco
US War Department decrees that Kansas and Tennessee are to be canvassed for volunteers
Battle at Booneville Mississippi: Confederate superior power driven out
Battle of Malvern Hill [Poindexter's Farm], day 7 of 7 days battles, Union forces repel Confederate attack, Tactical Union victory (US Civil War)
The Revenue Act of 1862 establishes first US income tax (3% of income over $600, 5% of income over $10,000), and a variety of excise taxes on goods, in order to fund the Civil War is signed into law
The Russian State Library is founded in Moscow
US Internal Revenue Law imposes 1st federal taxes on inheritance, tobacco and on incomes over $600 (progressive rate)
Free city delivery of mail begins in 49 US cities; postage 3 cents per oz
Slavery abolished in Dutch territories including Sint Maarten, Suriname and the Dutch Antilles, (now marked as Keti Koti Day in Suriname and the Netherlands)
Dutch newspaper stamp tax repealed
US mint at Carson City, Nevada opens
James W. Smith of South Carolina is one of 1st African Americans to attend West Point
The decimal currency system is made uniform in Canada
Prince Edward Island becomes 7th Canadian province
1st US kidnapping for ransom, 4-year-old Charles Ross, $20,000
1st zoo in the United States opens in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Dutch 1st Chamber accepts law governing child labor in factories
Universal Postal Union comes into effect after established in the Bern treaty, to organize international mail services
1st edition of "Amsterdammer" published
Canada joins the Universal Postal Union
Ex-khedive Ismael Pasha sails from Alexandria to Naples
1st international telephone conversation, Calais, Maine to St Stephen, New Brunswick
General Order 70, the culmination of the Cardwell-Childers reforms of the British Army's organisation, comes into effect
US Assay Office in St Louis, Missouri opens
ANWB forms in Utrecht, the Netherlands
The United States terminates reciprocity and fishery agreement with Canada
US mint at Carson City, Nevada reopens
Canada and Bermuda are linked by telegraph cable
Great Britain and Germany sign Zanzibar-Helgoland Treaty
San Francisco Bay City Club opens 1st US bicycle race track, made of wood
Harbor of Ymuiden opens in the Netherlands
Bronx acquires Hutton Square
Gideon Society established to place bibles in hotels, is founded in Janesville, Wisconsin
San Francisco City Hall turned over to city, after 29 years of construction
Ranji (230) & Newham (153) add 344 for 7th wkt, Sussex v Essex

US Congress passes the Philippines Government Act, providing that the Philippines be governed by a commission appointed by the President, with consent from the senate; Filipinos are to be treated as citizens of their land, no the USA
Tour de France: Inaugural race begins in Montgeron, a south-eastern suburb of Paris
Willie Anderson becomes the first 2-time Western Open golf champion, beating fellow Scot Alex Smith by 4 strokes at Michigan's Kent CC
The Orange River Colony, known as the Orange Free State, is granted self-government by the British
"SOS" (· · · – – – · · ·) distress signal becomes the worldwide standard for help
Chicago's White Sox Park (later Comiskey Park) opens - St Louis Browns beat White Sox, 2-0
Union of South Africa becomes a dominion
Proclamation removes "Dei Gratia" (by the Grace of God) from Canadian coins
The German gunboat 'Panther' arrives off Agadir allegedly to protect German interests and nationals in Morocco
Serbia and Greece declare war on Bulgaria
Australia begins Commonwealth Lighthouse Service
Australian Survey Corps becomes part of Military Forces
Battle of the Somme: The British launch their planned offensive in the Somme valley, but the Germans have quietly been preparing for weeks.
British court martial for the Easter uprising
Coca-Cola brings current coke formula to the market

First of four fatal Jersey Shore shark attacks occurs at Beach Haven when Charles Vansant (25) bleeds to death, challenging previous scientific belief that sharks never harm humans and sparking popular fear over sharks, which persists today
257cm (100-inch) diameter mirror for telepscope is delivered to Mount Wilson Observatory, in Los Angeles, California
In contravention of state law, Robins (Dodgers) play their 1st Sunday MLB game in Brooklyn; owner and manager arrested but escape with a fine; Robins edge Philadelphia, 3-2
MLB Cincinnati Reds' Fred Toney pitches 2 complete games in doubleheader victories over Pittsburgh Pirates, allows 3 hits each game, fewest allowed by any pitcher winning 2 MLB games in one day
Racial strife in East St Louis, Illinois turn deadly, as two White police officers are killed while driving through a previously attacked neighborhood in an unmarked car
SVV Scheveningen soccer club is established in the Dutch seaside town
US 1st class postage drops from 3 cents to 2 cents
Sir Herbert Samuel takes over as high commissioner over Palestine, where Arab resistance to the British Mandate continues
St Louis Cardinals become tenants of their AL rival St Louis Browns, as they abandon Robison Field midway through the MLB season and return to Sportsman's Park
1st permanent radio network-AT&T (WEAF NY & WMAF Mass)
Dircet regular transcontinental airmail service between New York and San Francisco forms
SDAP wins 4 chairs in Dutch Second-Parliamentary election
Secretary of State for Dominion Affairs created in UK
Canada restores gold standard
US Immigration law of 1924 in effect
Great Britain signs accord for Independence of Iraq
Cleveland Municipal Stadium (MLB: Indians, NFL: Browns, 78,189 capacity) opens; demolished 1996
Ice vending machines introduced in Los Angeles 25 lbs, 15 cents
Trans African Railway in use (Benguela, Angola-Jadotville, Congo)
NY newspaper Evening Standard goes bankrupt
114°F (45.6°C), Las Animas, Colorado (state record, tied in 1954; broken in 2019)
German nazi regime declares that married women shouldn't work
Strauss & von Hofmannsthal's opera "Arabella," premieres at the Semperoper Opera House in Dresden, Germany
The Canadian Parliament suspends all Chinese immigration.
1st x-ray photo of entire body, taken by Arthur Fuchs of Eastman-Kodak Company in Rochester, New York
Brookfield Zoo opens in Chicago, Illinois
Algemeen Nederlands Persbureau (ANP, General Dutch Press Agency) forms in Amsterdam
AVRO radio broadcast studios in Hilversum opens
Watchfulness Committee forms in Amsterdam
Rev Martin Niemöller arrested in Nazi Germany for activities against the State
The South African Press Association (SAPA) is established as a non-governmental institution by South Africa's major newspapers to facilitate the sharing of news, both national and international
Australia refuses entry to Dutch Jewish refugees
Bulova Watch Company pays $9 for 1st ever television commercial, which airs during a broadcast of Brooklyn Dodgers-Philadelphia Phillies baseball game
Mammoth Cave National Park established with 45,310 acres, including world's largest cave network in Kentucky, US
WCBW-TV (now WCBS), channel 2 in New York City begins broadcasting
WNBT-TV (now WNBC), channel 4 in New York City begins broadcasting
WWII: German troops conquer Black Sea port city of Sevastopol, Crimea, after a 250 day battle; Russia takes it back in 1944
"Pay-as-you-go" - 1st withholding tax from paychecks
2,500+ killed in London/SE England by German flying bombs
German officer Claus von Stauffenberg promoted to colonel
US headquarter moves to Colombières Normandy
Von Rundstedt against Keitel: "Sign peace, idiots!"
55th Postmaster General: Robert E Hannegan of Mo takes office
Allies troop land on Balikpapan
US drops atom bomb on Bikini atoll (4th atomic explosion)
192m long passenger ship Willem Ruys (Achille Lauro) launched
Basketball Association of America (BAA), which later became the National Basketball Association (NBA), holds its inaugural college player draft
British Dominion Affairs office becomes Commonwealth Relations office
NYC subway fare goes to 10 cents, bus fare to 7 cents and combo fare at 12 cents
US President Harry Truman dedicates Idlewild Field (now Kennedy Airport), in Jamaica, Queens, New York City
WBRC TV channel 6 in Birmingham, AL (ABC) begins broadcasting
WCCO TV channel 4 in Minneapolis-St Paul, MN (CBS) begins broadcasting
1st 407 US soldiers flown to South Korea
NYC bus fare rises to 10 cents equal to subway fare, combo fare at 15 cents
WHBF TV channel 4 in Rock Island, IL (CBS) begins broadcasting

English architect Michael Ventris says he has solved one of the 20th century's greatest linguistic riddles, by deciphering Linear B in BBC interview. Is an ancient form of Greek on clay tablets from Minoan palace of Knossos.
KLAS TV channel 8 in Las Vegas, NV (CBS) begins broadcasting
KTVH (now KWCH) TV channel 12 in Hutchinson-Wichita, KS (CBS) begins
WDBO (now WCPX) TV channel 6 in Orlando, FL (CBS) begins broadcasting
KOTA TV channel 3 in Rapid City, SD (ABC/NBC) begins broadcasting
Ibrahim Hashiroe succeeds Said el-Moefti as premier of Jordan
International Geophysical Year begins; 18-month scientific project lasts until Dec 31, 1958
WRLP TV channel 32 in Greenfield/Keene/Brat, Massachusetts (IND) begins broadcasting
The Canadian Broadcasting Corporation links television broadcasting across Canada via microwave
Heinrich Lubke elected President of West Germany
Israeli Knesset agrees to weapon sales to West Germany
The Party of the African Federation (PFA) holds its constitutive conference.
World Refugee Year begins
WVTV TV channel 18 in Milwaukee, WI (IND) begins broadcasting
First Nations peoples in Canada granted the right to vote in Federal elections, while still retaining their status and treaty rights
Ghana becomes a republic
No passports needed inside Benelux
Robert Bolt's "A Man For All Seasons" premieres in London
The independent Somali Democratic Republic, commonly known as Somalia, is formed out of former British and Italian territories
USSR shoots down US RB-47 reconnaissance plane
Haleakala National Park forms in Hawaii
KNDU TV channel 25 in Richland-Pasco-Kennew, WA (NBC) 1st broadcast
Algeria votes for independence from France
Burundi & Rwanda gain independence from Belgium (National Days)
Roman GeneralVespasianis first proclaimed Emperor by troops in Egypt, during year of the four emperors
Roman GeneralTitusand his forces set up battering rams to assault the walls of Jerusalem
Thomas Moregoes on trial in England on charges of treason for refusing to acceptHenry VIII's supremacy over the Pope
Army of Protestant KingWilliam IIIof England defeats deposed Roman Catholic KingJames IIin the Battle of the Boyne at Oldbridge, Ireland
Congregation of Notre Dame of Montreal founded byMarguerite Bourgeoyscanonically constituted a community
Napoleon's fleet reaches Alexandria, Egypt
US PresidentAndrew Jacksonannounces to Congress bequest by James Smithson of 100,000 gold sovereigns to found Smithsonian Institution in Washington
Abdul-Medjid succeedsMahmud IIas Sultan of the Ottoman Empire
Joint reading ofCharles DarwinandAlfred Russel Wallace's papers on evolution to the Linnean Society renders audience awestruck and silent
Alexander IIof Russia grants Jews right to publish books
The Dominion of Canada is formed, comprising the provinces of New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Ontario and Quebec, withJohn A. Macdonaldserving as the first Prime Minister
Henry Ossian Flipperof Georgia enters West Point Military Academy - later the first African American to graduate
Charles Taze Russellpublishes the first edition of the illustrated religious magazine "The Watchtower"
Elihu Root, US Attorney for the Southern District of New York (since 1883), submits his resignation to PresidentGrover Clevelandafter successfully prosecuting James C. Fish
Frederick Douglassnamed US Minister to Haiti
Wilfrid Lauriersworn in as 1st French speaking Prime Minister of Canada
Theodore Rooseveltand his Rough Riders charge up San Juan Hill during US's successful assault on the city of Santiago
GeneralJ. B. M. Hertzogfounds the Free State National Party
Pittsburgh shortstopHonus Wagner, at 42 and 4 months, connects at Cincinnati to become the oldest to hit an inside-the-park HR; Pirates beat Reds, 2-1
Washington Senators pitching legendWalter Johnsonno-hits Boston Red Sox 1-0 at Fenway Park, the only no-hitter of his illustrious career
Belgian scouting magazine Le Boy Scout Belge publishes "The Adventures of Totor" by cartoonistHergé, his first comic strip series and a precursor to Tintin
US PresidentHerbert Hooverplaces a one year moratorium on war debt payments
NY GovernorFranklin D. Rooseveltnominated for president at Democratic National Convention in Chicago, Illinois
Spanish bishops supportFrancisco Francoand his fascist movement
New York Yankees outfielderJoe DiMaggio, on way to a record 56, ties Willie Keeler's 44 game MLB hit streak in a 9-2 win over Boston Red Sox at Yankee Stadium
Bretton Woods Conference starts, dominated by Harry Dexter White andJohn Maynard Keynesand leads to establishment of the IMF and the World Bank
GeneralEisenhowervisits front in Normandy
First MLB superstar to return from WW II,Hank Greenberghomers in his 1st game (Detroit beats Philadelphia A's 9-5)
The last White Rajah,Charles Vyner Brookecedes Sarawak to the British Crown
Brooklyn's future Baseball Hall of Fame catcherRoy Campanelladebuts with 3 hits in a 6-4 loss to the rival NY Giants

"Magna Carta of the Information Age" "A Mathematical Theory of Communication", article of book byClaude Shannonand Warren Weaver laying foundation for information theory, published in "Scientific American"
Cleveland Indians veteran hurlerBob Fellerpitches his 3rd career no-hitter beating Detroit Tigers, 2-1
Elvis Presley, wearing a tuxedo, sings to a basset hound on TheSteve AllenShow
Benjamin Britten's cantata "Carmen Baseliense" premieres in Basel, Switzerland
Fidel Castronationalizes Esso, Shell & Texaco in Cuba
1st vote on Declaration of Independence for Britain's North American colonies
Battle of Gettysburg begins in Pennsylvania, Union forces halt Confederate GeneralRobert E. Lee's Army of Northern Virginia
III Summer (Modern) Olympic Games open in St Louis, the first held in the United States
First day of the Battle of the Somme in World War I: The British Army suffers its worst day, losing 19,240 men
St. Laurence Justinian [Lorenzo Giustiniani], Italian catholic bishop and 1st Patriarch of Venice, born in Venice, Republic of Venice (d. 1456)
Louis II, King of Hungary and Bohemia (1516-26), born in Buda, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1526)
Hadrianus Junius [Adriaen de Jonghe], Dutch physician, historian and humanist, born in Hoorn, Netherlands (d. 1575)
Frederick II, King of Denmark and Norway (1559-88), born in Haderslevhus Castle, Haderslev, Denmark (d. 1588)
Charles III de Croÿ, Prince of Chimay and Duke of Aarschot, born in Beaumont, Hainaut (d. 1612)
Joseph Hall, English bishop and writer (Virgidemiarum: Six Books), born in Ashby-de-la-Zouch, England (d. 1656)
Claudio Saracini, Italian composer, born in Siena, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1630)
Johann Heinrich Heidegger, Swiss theologian, born in Bäretswil, Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1698)
Franz Xaver Murschhauser, German composer and theorist, born in Saverne, Alsace, France (d. 1738)
Anthony Collins, English philosopher (A Dscourse On Free-Thinking), born in Hounslow, Middlesex, England (d. 1729) [birth date June 21 O.S.]
Johann Ludwig Steiner, Swiss composer, born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1761)
Pedro Rodríguez de Campomanes y Pérez, 1st Count of Campomanes, Spanish economist and literary, born in Tineo, Asturias, Kingdom of Spain (d. 1803)
James Lyon, American composer, born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1794)
Georg Christoph Lichtenberg, German physicist and writer (types of Lichtenberg), born in Ober-Ramstadt, Landgraviate of Hesse-Darmstadt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1799)
Georg Christoph Grosheim, German composer, born in Kasel, Duchy of Luxembourg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1841)
Jean-Victor Poncelet, French mathematician and engineer who was one of the founders of modern projective geometry, born in Metz, France (d. 1867)
Gideon Welles, 24th U.S. Secretary of the Navy under Presidents Abraham Lincoln and Andrew Johnson, born in Glastonbury, Connecticut (d. 1878)
George Sand [Amantine-Lucile-Aurore Dudevant], French novelist (Valentine, Le Figaro), born in Paris, France (d. 1876)
Thomas Green Clemson, American politician and founder of Clemson University, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1888)
Willem Jan d'Ablaing van Giessenburg, Dutch genealogist and heraldicus, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1892)
Ignaz Semmelweis, Hungarian physician who discovered the cause of puerperal (childbed) fever and introduced antisepsis into medical practice, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1865)
Alfred Thomas Archimedes Torbert, Bvt Major General (Union Army), born in Georgetown, Delaware (d. 1880)
Jadwiga Łuszczewska, Polish poet, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1908)
Robert Stawell Ball, Irish mathematician and astronomer (Ball Screw), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1913)
Verney Lovett Cameron, English explorer (Tanganyika), born in Radipole, Dorset, England (d. 1894)
John Selby, England cricket batsman (6 Tests, 2 x 50; Nottinghamshire CCC), born in Nottingham, England (d. 1894)
Martha Hughes Cannon, American politician, polygamous wife and women's rights activist (1st US female state senator, Utah 1897-1901), born in  Llandudno, Wales (d. 1932)
Roger Connor, American Baseball Hall of Fame 1st baseman (career HR record 138 stood for 23 years; NL batting champion 1885; NL RBI leader 1889; NY Giants), born in Waterbury, Connecticut (d. 1931)
Willard Metcalf, American painter known chiefly for his Impressionist landscape work, and instructor, born in Lowell, Massachusetts (d. 1925)

John Clarkson, American Baseball HOF pitcher (Triple Crown 1889; no-hitter 1885; 3 × NL wins leader; 3 × NL strikeout leader; Chicago White Stockings, Boston Beaneaters), born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 1909)
Samuel D. Riddle, American thoroughbred owner (Man 'o War, War Admiral), born in Glen Riddle, Pennsylvania (d. 1951)

William Grant Stairs, Canadian-British explorer and adventurer who had a leading role in two of the most controversial expeditions in the history of the colonisation of Africa, born in Halifax, Nova Scotia (d. 1892)
William Strunk Jr., American grammarian and author (The Elements of Style), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1946)
Louis Blériot, French aviator and 1st man to fly an airplane across English Channel, born in Cambrai, France (d. 1936)
Alice Guy-Blaché, French film director who was the first woman to direct a film (The Fairy of the Cabbages), born in Paris (d. 1968)
Leon Jouhaux, French socialist and co-founder UN's ILO (Nobel 1951), born in Paris (d. 1954)
Piet[er] L. Kramer, Influential Dutch architect (Amsterdam School), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1961)
Arthur Borton, English soldier and recipient of the Victoria Cross, born in Cheveney, Kent (d. 1933)
Charles D. Brown, American actor (Barefoot Boy, Disbarred, Night Editor), born in Council Bluffs, Iowa (d. 1948)
Ben Taylor, American Baseball Hall of Fame 1st baseman (Indianapolis ABCs) and manager (Washington Potomacs, Baltimore Black Sox), born in Anderson, South Carolina (d. 1953)
James M. Cain, American novelist (Postman Always Rings Twice), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1977)
Walter Francis White, American civil rights activist who led the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1955)
Pavel Antokolsky, Russian poet and theatre director (Syn), born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1978)
"Georgia" Tom [Dorsey], American blues and gospel pianist, songwriter ("Peace In The Valley"), and evangelist, born in Villa Rica, Georgia (d. 1993)
Cavan O'Connor, British singer ("I'm Only a Strolling Vagabond"), born in Nottingham, England (d. 1997)
Konstantinos Tsatsos, Greek politician and 2nd President of Greece (1975-1980), born in Athens, Greece (d. 1987)
Irna Phillips, American scriptwriter who created 6 soap operas (Guiding Light), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1973)
Sylvia Mary Paget Chancellor, English philanthropist (Prisoners' Wives Service), born in London (d. 1996)
José Luis Sert, Spanish-American architect and urban developer, born in Barcelona (d. 1983)
Beatrix Lehmann, British actress (Candles at Nine; Staircase), born in Bourne End, Buckinghamshire, England (d. 1979)
Mary Calderone, American physician and founder (planned parenthood), born in New York City (d. 1998)
Bill Stern, American sportscaster (first televised college football game), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1971)
Ed Gordon Jr, American athlete (Olympic gold long jump 1932), born in Jackson, Mississippi (d. 1971)
Peter Anders, German opera singer, born in Essen, Germany (d. 1954)
Juan Carlos Onetti, Uruguayan novelist (La vida breve), born in Montevideo, Uruguay (d. 1994)
Madge Evans, American TV panelist (Masquerade Party), born in New York City (d. 1981)
Vera Brodsky Lawrence, American concert pianist (CBS Symphony) and musicologist (Joplin; Gottschalk), born in Norfolk, Virginia (d. 1996)
Wesley Bolin, American politician (Governor of Arizona, 1977-78; Secretary of State of Arizona, 1949-77), born near Butler, Missouri (d. 1978)
Glen Hardin, American athlete (Olympic gold 1936 400m hurdles), born in Derma, Mississippi (d. 1975)
Alvino Rey, American orchestra leader (King Family), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2004)
Sergey Sokolov, Russian-Soviet marshal and Minister of Defence of the Soviet Union (1984-87), born in Yevpatoria, Russian Empire (d. 2012)
Ahmad Hassan al-Bakr, Iraqi fieldmarshal and President of Iraq (1968-79), born in Tikrit, Ottoman Empire (d. 1982)
David Brower, American environmentalist and president (Sierra Club), born in Berkeley, California (d. 2000)
Sally Kirkland, American fashion editor (Vogue magazine) and manager (Lord & Taylor), born in El Reno, Canadian County, Oklahoma (d. 1989)
Jo Sinclair [Ruth Seid], American writer (Wasteland), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1995)
Christl Cranz, German alpine skier (combined gold Winter Olympics 1936), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 2004)
Earle Warren, American alto sax player, born in Springfield, Ohio (d. 1994)
Jean Stafford, American writer (Pulitzer Prize 1970-The Collected Stories of Jean Stafford), born in Covina, California (d. 1979)
Joseph Ransohoff, American neurosurgeon and pioneer in the modern neurosurgery, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 2001)
Willie Dixon, American blues musician ("Hoochie Coochie Man"), born in Vicksburg, Mississippi (d. 1992)
Bob Prince, American sportscaster (Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1985)

Tadkeshwar, British India (d. 2005)
Ralph Young, American pop singer (Sandler and Young), born in New York City (d. 2008)
Harold Sakata [Toshiyuki], American actor (Gold Finger, Dimension 5), born in Holualoa, Hawaii (d. 1982)

Don Whitmire, American College Football Hall of Fame tackle (Alabama, Navy - All American at both), born in Giles County, Tennessee (d. 1991)
Constance Ford, American model and actress (Burden Hunt, Another World), born in New York City (d. 1993)
Ruth Olay, American jazz and cabaret singer, born in San Francisco, California (d. 2021)

Frank Lowson, England cricket batsman (7 Tests, 2 x 50s; Yorkshire CCC), born in Bradford, England (d. 1984)
Carl Hahn, German automotive executive (CEO of Volkswagen Group), born in Chemnitz, Saxony, Germany (d. 2023)
Delmar Watson, American child actor (Mr. Smith Goes to Washington, Heidi, Annie Oakley), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2008)
François-Régis Bastide, French writer and broadcaster, born in Biarritz, France (d. 1996)
Hans Werner Henze, German composer, born in Gütersloh, Westphalia, Germany (d. 2012)
Robert Fogel, American economic historian (Nobel Prize 1993), born in New York City (d. 2013)

Hans Eklund, Swedish composer, born in Sandviken, Sweden (d. 1999)
Sam Denoff, American screenwriter (The Dick Van Dyke Show) and television producer (That Girl), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2011)[1]

Gerald Edelman, American biochemist (Nobel 1972), born in Ozone Park, New York (d. 2014)

Moustapha Akkad, Syrian-American film producer and director (Lion of the Dessert), born in Aleppo, French Mandate for Syria and the Lebanon (d. 2005)

Chris Strachwitz, German-born American folk, roots, and world music record producer and label founder (Arhoolie Records), born in Gross Reichenau, Lower Silesia, Germany (now Bogaczów, Poland) (d. 2023)[1]

Bobby Day [Robert Byrd], American rock vocalist ("Rockin' Robin"), born in Fort Worth, Texas (d. 1990)

Peter Walwyn, British thoroughbred trainer (champion trainer 1974-75), born in Lambourn, England (d. 2017)
Sam Rutigliano, American football coach (Cleveland Browns 1978-84) and broadcaster (WEWS-TV Cleveland), born in Brooklyn, New York
Claude Berri, French actor, director and screenwriter, born in Paris (d. 2009)

Jean Marsh, British Emmy Award-winning stage and screen actress (Upstairs, Downstairs - "Rose"; The Eagle Has Landed), born in London (d. 2025)
Dave Prowse, English actor (Darth Vader), born in Bristol. England (d. 2020)
James Cotton, American blues vocalist and harmonica player, born in Tunica, Mississippi (d. 2017)

Syl Johnson [SylvesterThompson], American blues and soul singer-songwriter, guitarist, and record producer ("Different Strokes", "Is It Because I'm Black"), born near Holly Springs, Mississippi (d. 2022)
Wally Amos, American cookie entrepreneur (Famous Amos), television personality, adult literacy advocate, and motivational speaker, born in Tallahassee, Florida (d. 2024)[1]
Ebrahim Ismail Ebrahim, South African ANCer and Umkhonto we Sizwe-leader, born in Durban, South Africa
Chester Watson, West Indian cricket fast bowler (7 Tests, 19 wickets; Jamaica, Delhi), born in Negril, Jamaica
Hariprasad Chaurasia, Indian Hindustani classical flautist, bansuri player, and film score composer, born in Allahabad, British India
Charles Floyd Hatcher, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Georgia), born in Doerun, Georgia
Delaney Bramlett, American rocker (Shindogs; Delaney & Bonnie), born in Pontotoc, Mississippi (d. 2008)
Frank Parker, American actor (Days of our Lives, Never Too Young), born in Darby, Pennsylvania (2018)
Karen Black, American actress (5 Easy Pieces, Pyx), born in Park Ridge, Illinois (d. 2013)
Craig Brown, Scottish soccer wing half (Rangers, Dundee, Falkirk) and manager (Scotland 1993-2001; Preston NE, Motherwell, Aberdeen), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 2023)
John Gould, British songwriter and composer for musical theater, born in Newquay, England
Alfred G. Gilman, American scientist (1994 Nobel laureate for G-protein discoveries), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 2015)
Myron Scholes, Canadian-American financial economist (Nobel 1997), born in Timmins, Ontario
Rod Gilbert, Canadian Hockey HOF right-wing (NY Rangers; first NYR to have # retired), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2021)
